mnemonics.title: HEAP Steps
mnemonics.content: HEAP = Heapify, Extract, Arrange, Process again. Remember: First BUILD-MAX-HEAP, then repeatedly swap root with last element and heapify the reduced heap.

mnemonics.title: Parent Children Index
mnemonics.content: PARENT(i) = floor(i/2), LEFT(i) = 2i, RIGHT(i) = 2i+1. Mnemonic: 'PARE' → PARE-left-right: Parent is i/2, Left is double, Right is double-plus-one.

mnemonics.title: Heapify Direction
mnemonics.content: MAX-HEAP: Parent ≥ Children. MIN-HEAP: Parent ≤ Children. Mnemonic: 'MAX goes UP, MIN goes DOWN' - In max-heap, largest element bubbles up to root.

mnemonics.title: Linear Time Sorting Trio
mnemonics.content: CRB = Counting, Radix, Bucket sort - the three linear time sorting algorithms (O(n+k), O(d(n+k)), O(n)). Remember: 'Counting Counts, Radix Radically, Bucket Basics' all beat O(n log n) comparison sorts.

mnemonics.title: Heapsort Complexity
mnemonics.content: Time: O(n log n) for all cases (best, average, worst). Space: O(1) auxiliary. Mnemonic: 'HEAPSORT = HIGH EFFICIENCY ALL PAGES SORTING TOO' → HEAP: High Efficiency All Pairs, SORT: Stable, O(n log n).

acronyms.usage: Remember the two main phases of heapsort: building the heap and extracting elements one by one.
Used for ascending order sort; root contains maximum element.
Used for descending order sort or priority queues; root contains minimum element.
The three linear time sorting algorithms that achieve O(n) time under certain conditions.

acronyms.expansion: Build Maximum Heap from unsorted array
acronyms.usage: First phase of heapsort; transforms unsorted array into max-heap in O(n) time.

acronyms.expansion: Index relationships in array representation of binary heap
acronyms.usage: Used to navigate the tree structure stored in array; essential for implementing heapify.

visualTips.concept: Binary Heap as Complete Binary Tree
visualTips.visualization: Picture a perfectly filled binary tree from left to right (like a pyramid with no gaps). The array indices fill level by level: root at index 1, then indices 2-3, then 4-7, etc. No holes allowed!

Imagine a faulty parent element that is smaller than its children (in max-heap). It 'sinks' down by swapping with the larger child repeatedly until it's in the right place - like a heavy object sinking in water.

Visualize: (1) Build a mountain (max-heap) from scattered blocks. (2) Take the top golden block (max), put it at the end. (3) Rebuild the mountain slightly smaller. (4) Repeat until mountain is gone - now you have sorted blocks!

Imagine a hotel with rooms numbered 0 to k. Each guest (input element) goes to their room number. Then simply walk through rooms in order to collect sorted guests. Room count tells you how many of each value.

Think of sorting postal packages: first sort by last digit (units) into 10 buckets, then stack them back. Then sort by tens digit, then hundreds, etc. Each pass preserves previous order (stable sort).

analogies.analogy: Think of a company's hierarchy where the CEO (root) always earns more than all managers (children), who earn more than all employees (leaves). A max-heap ensures the biggest value 'rules' at the top.
Like sediment sinking in a glass of water - heavier particles (larger values) sink to the bottom while lighter ones float up. The sift-down operation makes larger elements sink down the heap.

Heapsort is like systematically removing the tallest person from a group and lining them up, while QuickSort is like randomly picking a pivot and partitioning around it. Heapsort guarantees performance; QuickSort is faster on average but can degrade.

Like organizing books by grade: all 'A' grade books go on shelf A, all 'B' books on shelf B. No comparing books to each other - just put each in its designated shelf, then read shelves in order. That's O(n + range) time!
Like alphabetically sorting files by first sorting by last name, then within those groups sorting by middle name, then first name - each pass uses only one digit/letter, making it simple and linear.

In-place heapsort is like sorting books on a shelf without using any extra table space - you just rearrange them in place. QuickSort also does this, but MergeSort needs a temporary table (extra space).
